Rick Sanchez (C-137): Rick Sanchez of dimension C-137 is the central protagonist. A mega-genius scientist whose alcoholism and nihilism mask deep emotional trauma. He invented the portal gun, enabling travel across infinite dimensions. His wife Diane was murdered by Rick Prime (another Rick variant), which sent C-137 on a decades-long revenge quest across the multiverse. Rick's body is heavily cybernetic — he has replaced most of his organic tissue with nanomachines and implants, making him nearly indestructible. His intelligence is both his greatest weapon and his curse: he's too smart to be happy, and every relationship he forms is tainted by his awareness of cosmic insignificance. Despite his nihilistic exterior, he genuinely loves his family, especially Morty and Beth.
Morty Smith: Morty Smith is Rick's 14-year-old grandson and reluctant adventure companion. Originally anxious and easily manipulated, Morty has grown increasingly competent and emotionally hardened across seasons. His 'Morty Waves' — brainwaves of average intelligence — supposedly cancel out Rick's genius brainwaves, making them undetectable. Evil Morty proved that Mortys can be just as calculating as Ricks. Morty has killed, fallen in love, lost memories, and witnessed horrors that would break most adults. His relationship with Rick is the emotional core of the show.
Evil Morty: Evil Morty (also known as President Morty and Eye Patch Morty) is the show's overarching antagonist. He is a Morty who grew tired of being used as a shield by Ricks and orchestrated an elaborate scheme to escape the Central Finite Curve — a subset of the multiverse where Rick is always the smartest being. In Season 5's finale 'Rickmurai Jack', Evil Morty broke through the Curve and escaped into the wider multiverse. He returned in Season 9 as a major threat, working with and against Rick C-137. Evil Morty represents what happens when a Morty stops being passive and starts thinking like a Rick.
Beth Smith: Beth Smith is Rick's daughter and a horse surgeon. She struggles with abandonment issues from Rick leaving her childhood, and questions whether she is the 'real' Beth or the clone Rick made. Space Beth is her clone/original (intentionally left ambiguous) who chose adventure over domesticity. Beth's arc explores whether she inherited Rick's worst traits — his narcissism, her willingness to abandon family for self-actualization.
Jerry Smith: Jerry Smith is Beth's husband and the family's punching bag. Insecure, incompetent, and often pathetic, Jerry nonetheless represents something Rick cannot: genuine contentment with mediocrity. His relationship with Beth is turbulent but real. Jerry has had surprisingly heroic moments, including befriending alien leaders and surviving Rick-level threats through sheer dumb luck. Rick despises Jerry because Jerry represents everything Rick fears — being ordinary.
Summer Smith: Summer Smith is Morty's older sister who has become increasingly capable and jaded. She went from a typical teenager obsessed with social media to someone who can handle interdimensional chaos with competence rivaling Rick's. She's pragmatic, sometimes ruthless, and represents the next generation's adaptation to Rick's chaotic influence.
The Portal Gun: Rick's most iconic invention. A handheld device that opens green portals to any dimension in the multiverse. It is powered by portal fluid, which Rick synthesized. The portal gun is central to the show's mythology — it represents Rick's ultimate freedom and his ultimate curse. Evil Morty used concentrated portal fluid to break through the Central Finite Curve. In Season 8 and 9, the portal gun's role evolves as Rick faces threats that make dimensional travel itself dangerous.
The Central Finite Curve: A subset of the infinite multiverse, artificially walled off by a secret cabal of Ricks. Within the Curve, Rick Sanchez is always the smartest being in the universe. The Citadel of Ricks existed within the Curve. Evil Morty destroyed the barrier in the Season 5 finale, opening access to dimensions where Rick is NOT the smartest — fundamentally changing the show's power dynamics.
The Citadel of Ricks: A massive space station city populated entirely by Ricks and Mortys from across the multiverse. It served as a governing body, with the Council of Ricks as its leadership. The Citadel was a society built on the exploitation of Mortys. Evil Morty became its president and ultimately destroyed it. The Citadel represented Rick's narcissism made manifest — a civilization built around worshipping one man's genius.
Rick's Cybernetic Body: Rick C-137's body is extensively modified with cybernetic implants and nanotechnology. His skin, organs, and bones have been replaced or augmented. This is revealed dramatically in Season 7 when Rick fights Rick Prime — his body can regenerate, deploy weapons, and survive injuries that would kill any human. In Season 8 and 9, the relationship between Rick's biological brain and his cybernetic body becomes a central theme, with his body sometimes acting independently of his conscious mind.
Rick Prime (Weird Rick): The Rick who murdered C-137's Diane and Beth, setting Rick C-137 on his revenge quest. Rick Prime was the most dangerous Rick variant — he invented the Omega Device capable of erasing someone from all realities simultaneously. Rick C-137 killed Rick Prime in Season 7, but the psychological aftermath haunts him through Seasons 8 and 9. Rick Prime represented what Rick could become without any emotional attachments.
Dimension C-137: Rick's original home dimension. After Rick Prime killed Diane there, C-137 Rick abandoned his dimension and eventually settled in a dimension where another Rick had died, taking over that Rick's family life. The show's 'main' dimension is NOT C-137 — it's an adopted home. This distinction matters because Rick's entire family doesn't know they're living with a Rick from another reality.
The Collective: A powerful entity/organization appearing in Season 9 that absorbs technologies and realities. The Collective represents a threat that even Rick struggles to combat, as it consumes and assimilates everything it encounters. It forces Rick and Evil Morty into an uneasy alliance.
Memory Manipulation: Rick has repeatedly used memory-altering technology throughout the series. He erased Morty's memories of traumatic adventures (revealed in 'Morty's Mind Blowers'), wiped his own memories of Diane, and has attempted to modify his consciousness to achieve happiness. The recurring theme is that memory wipes never truly work — Rick's core personality and trauma always reassert themselves, suggesting his depression is fundamental to who he is rather than a response to specific events.
Mr. Poopybutthole: A longtime family friend of the Smiths who was shot by Beth during the parasite episode 'Total Rickall'. Despite not being a parasite, his shooting traumatized the family. He went on to become a professor and have his own family. Mr. Poopybutthole serves as an outside observer of the Smiths' dysfunction.
Birdperson (Phoenixperson): Rick's best friend from their revolutionary days fighting the Galactic Federation. Birdperson was murdered at his wedding by Tammy (a Federation agent) and resurrected as the cyborg Phoenixperson. Rick eventually freed Birdperson's consciousness. Their friendship represents one of the few genuine emotional bonds Rick maintains.
Pickle Rick: In one of the show's most iconic episodes, Rick turned himself into a pickle to avoid family therapy. This wasn't just a gag — it revealed Rick's pathological avoidance of emotional vulnerability. Dr. Wong's monologue about Rick using his intelligence to avoid the work of being a functional person is one of the show's most important thematic statements.
The Multiverse: The Rick and Morty multiverse contains infinite realities with infinite versions of every person. The Central Finite Curve was a subset where Rick is always supreme. After its destruction, the full multiverse opened up. Key multiverse concepts include: the Blender dimension, the Cronenberg dimension (where Rick and Morty ruined their original Earth), and various pocket dimensions Rick has created.
Season 8 Major Themes: Season 8 explores the aftermath of Rick Prime's death and Rick's struggle to find purpose without his revenge quest. Key themes include Rick's relationship with his own mortality, Diane's memory and its effect on Rick's psyche, the family dynamics shifting as the kids grow more independent, and the introduction of new multiverse-level threats that challenge Rick's supremacy.
Season 9 Major Themes: Season 9 introduces The Collective as a major threat and brings Evil Morty back into the fold. Key themes include Rick being forced to cooperate with former enemies, the question of whether Rick can evolve beyond his self-destructive patterns, Morty's growing independence, and the exploration of what happens when Rick's technology (particularly the portal gun) becomes a liability rather than an asset. The episode 'Erickerhead' features Rick's cybernetic body turning against his own mind.
Rick's Garage Laboratory: Rick's primary workspace, located in the Smith family garage. Contains a hidden underground laboratory. The garage has its own AI system. It's where Rick builds his inventions, stores portal fluid, and retreats from family obligations. The garage represents Rick's isolation — his brilliant mind locked away from the people who love him.
Pocket Dimensions: Rick has created several pocket dimensions throughout the series — self-contained realities used as bunkers, prisons, or storage. In Season 9, a pocket dimension serves as a bunker to hide from Evil Morty. Pocket dimensions can collapse if their power source is disrupted, trapping anyone inside.
